title: Azerbaijan Destroys New Armenian Church in Artsakh, Experts Say
summary:
Imagine a church that was built to be a safe place — a bomb shelter during war — is now completely gone. That's what happened to the Holy Mother of God Church in Stepanakert, a city in Artsakh (also called Nagorno-Karabakh).

Satellite pictures taken by a group called Caucasus Heritage Watch (CHW) show the church was demolished (torn down) in the last two months. The church was only finished in 2019, after 12 years of building. During the war in 2020, people hid inside it from bombs. Now it's rubble.

Azerbaijan, the country that now controls Artsakh, has been destroying Armenian churches and cemeteries since 2023, when over 130,000 Armenians were forced to leave their homes. Experts call this "cultural genocide" — destroying a group's history and culture on purpose.

This news comes just three days before the 111th anniversary of the Armenian Genocide (when 1.5 million Armenians were killed by the Ottoman Empire in 1915). Turkey and Azerbaijan still say it never happened.

Armenia's prime minister, Nikol Pashinyan, is trying to make peace with Azerbaijan. He says talking about Artsakh is dangerous for peace. But many Armenians are sad and angry about losing their churches and their homeland.
why_it_matters: This story shows that preserving history and culture is important, and that war can destroy not just buildings but also people's sense of belonging.

keywords.explanation: A country in the Caucasus region, neighbor of Armenia
A church built by Armenian Christians, part of their cultural heritage
Another name for Nagorno-Karabakh, a region in the Caucasus
Destroying a group's history, art, and buildings on purpose

questions.question: What did the church serve as during the 2020 war?
How many Armenians were forced to leave their homes in 2023?
What is Prime Minister Pashinyan trying to do?

background_read: Artsakh (Nagorno-Karabakh) is a region in the Caucasus mountains that has been fought over for decades.
Many Armenian churches and monasteries there are hundreds of years old, and destroying them is seen as trying to erase Armenian history.
The Armenian Genocide of 1915 killed 1.5 million Armenians; many countries recognize it as a genocide, but Turkey and Azerbaijan do not.
Article_Structure: WHO: Azerbaijan (the country that now controls Artsakh)
WHAT: Destroyed the Holy Mother of God Church in Stepanakert
WHERE: Stepanakert, the capital city of Artsakh
WHY: Experts say it is part of a pattern of cultural genocide to remove Armenian heritage from the region

perspectives.description: Azerbaijan says it is restoring its own territory and removing symbols of a past conflict.
Armenians and many experts see this as cultural genocide — destroying churches and cemeteries to erase Armenian history.
The destruction of religious buildings in conflicts is common; it often deepens hatred and makes peace harder.
